posts.text:
I put on a yellow rubber jumpsuit and a respirator.

Crawled under my house on my hands and knees.

Found a rotting opossum.

And in that moment I understood something about procrastination I'd been getting wrong for years.

The smell had been there for days.

I explored every other option. Found mushrooms under the deck. Tried to disqualify those.

Tried to ignore it entirely.

The smell didn't care.

And every day I didn't deal with it, the dread got heavier.

Here's what actually happened by avoiding it:

The task didn't get smaller.

The dead opossum didn't decompose faster because I was anxious about it.

All I did was spend days accumulating guilt and worry on top of a task that was already going to be awful.

I made it worse by waiting.

The quest itself took maybe 45 minutes.

The avoidance cost me days of background dread.

That's the trade most of us are making without realizing it.

The task has a fixed cost. The avoidance is interest on a loan you didn't have to take.

After I buried it in the greenbelt in a ripped yellow jumpsuit looking like a crime scene, I took a shower. Watched a movie with my wife.

And the weight lifted immediately.

The task wasn't just physical. It had an emotional cost that needed a recovery plan.

That's the part nobody talks about.

The insight that came out of all of it:

Every hard task has two costs.

The actual cost of doing it.
The emotional cost of what it takes out of you.

If you plan for both, you can do almost anything.

If you only plan for the first one, you burn out wondering why.

The dead opossum taught me that.

Delayed tasks collect dread. The task itself was always smaller than the waiting.

label: Crawling under the house to find a dead opossum as a real-life illustration of why avoided tasks cost more than the tasks themselves

posts.text:
Before you start a task you've been avoiding, ask yourself one question:

What does this make me feel?

Not what is it. Not how long will it take.

What does it make you FEEL.

The answer changes everything about how you approach it.

Most productivity systems treat tasks like math.

Input time + effort = output.

But some tasks cost more than time.

They cost disgust. Anxiety. Overwhelm. Dread.

If your plan doesn't account for the emotional cost, the plan is incomplete.

And you'll wonder why you couldn't just push through.

Here's a simple framework I'm building into the game:

Before a hard task, name:
→ What emotion is blocking me?
→ What's the first survivable step?
→ What do I do immediately after to recover?

That third question is the one most people skip.

It's also the most important one.

The recovery task is not a reward.

It's maintenance.

I crawled under my house, found a dead opossum, and buried it in the greenbelt.

Then I took a shower and watched a movie with my wife.

That wasn't a treat. That was how I stayed functional the next day.

Plan the recovery before you start. Not after.

Try it with the task you've been avoiding longest:

1. Name the feeling blocking you
2. Find the smallest survivable first step
3. Decide what you do the moment it's done

You're not optimizing. You're just making the emotional cost visible before it surprises you.

Visible costs are manageable. Invisible ones are not.

label: How to map the emotional cost of a dreaded task before you start it so you can plan for recovery, not just completion

posts.text:
Productivity culture has one answer for tasks you avoid:

Just do it.

Broken advice. And here's why.

Avoidance doesn't freeze the task.

It runs a tab.

Every day you don't do the thing, you pay a little more in background anxiety, guilt, and dread.

By the time you finally do it, you've already spent more than the task was worth.

And the task itself? It was always the same size.

The actual problem isn't willpower.

It's that most tasks that get avoided have an emotional cost nobody accounted for.

Difficult. Disgusting. Overwhelming. Scary.

If your system doesn't make that emotional cost visible before you start, it's not a system.

It's just a to-do list with judgment attached.

The fix isn't trying harder.

It's planning for both costs:
→ The task itself
→ What the task takes out of you

When you plan a recovery after a hard thing, hard things become completable.

When you don't, you burn out and blame your character.

That's a design failure, not a personal one.

Avoidance is not laziness. It is an unplanned emotional cost running in the background with no off switch.

Name it. Plan around it. Do the thing.

The dread will always be bigger than the task.

Every single time.

label: The tasks you avoid don't get cheaper — avoidance charges interest in the form of dread, and most productivity systems ignore that entirely

posts.text:
I used to avoid hard tasks by not thinking about them.

Out of sight. Out of mind.

Except they were never actually out of mind.

They were running in the background eating energy I didn't know I was spending.

Here's what changed:

I stopped trying to ignore the emotional weight of hard tasks.

And started naming it instead.

What does this make me feel?

Anxious. Disgusted. Overwhelmed. Dreading.

Just naming it out loud reduces its hold. Research backs this.

Dr. Dan Siegel calls it name it to tame it.

I'm building this into the game as a mechanic.

Each hard task gets mapped onto a monster.

Not to be cute. But because the monster gives the emotion somewhere to live outside your head.

You're not fighting yourself anymore.

You're fighting the Undead Ghoul that represents the anxiety and disgust around this specific task.

The monster mechanic does three things:

→ Names the emotional cost before you start
→ Gives you something external to push against
→ Forces you to plan a recovery before the task consumes you

It turns avoidance into strategy.

That's the only version of this that actually works for me.

Past me avoided the dead opossum under the house for days.

Future me names it as the Undead Ghoul.

Plans for the disgust and anxiety it carries.

Decides on the shower and the movie after.

And goes in.

Same task. Completely different relationship to it.

The monster didn't make it easier. It made it possible.

label: How naming a hard task as a monster with a face changed the relationship from avoidance to something actionable

posts.text:
Dr. Dan Siegel has a phrase:

"Name it to tame it."

Label an emotion and you reduce its intensity in the brain almost immediately.

I spent years trying to push through hard tasks with willpower.

I should have been naming what I felt instead.

Here's the neuroscience in plain language:

When you're overwhelmed, the emotional brain is running the show.

Labeling the emotion activates the prefrontal cortex — the part that actually solves problems.

You don't logic your way out of dread.

You name your way out.

For ADHD brains this matters even more.

Dr. Russell Barkley describes ADHD as a deficit in self-regulation, not attention.

Tasks with high emotional cost trigger avoidance because the regulatory system needed to push through is already running at a deficit.

More dread = more avoidance = more dread.

The loop is automatic without intervention.

The intervention isn't willpower.

It's structure.

If the system asks you to name the emotion before you engage with the task, it does the regulatory work your brain skips.

External scaffolding replaces internal executive function.

That's the design principle the whole game is built on.

I'm building this into the game as the Quest Matrix.

Before a hard task: name the feeling, map it to a monster, plan the recovery.

It sounds like a lot of steps.

It's actually faster than three days of background dread eating your energy while you avoid the opossum under your house.

The science says name it. The game makes you do it.

label: Why naming emotions reduces their power, and how building that neuroscience into a game mechanic changes how ADHD brains handle dreaded tasks

posts.text:
This week a new mechanic made it into the game.

It came from a dead opossum, a ripped rubber jumpsuit, and a shovel full of maggots.

I'm not being dramatic.

This is actually how the game gets built.

The problem I was solving:

Some tasks aren't just hard. They carry an emotional cost that standard task systems completely ignore.

Disgust. Dread. Anxiety. Overwhelm.

If your system doesn't make those visible, they accumulate invisibly until you can't move.

The mechanic is called the Quest Matrix.

Before a hard task:
→ Name what you're feeling
→ Map it onto a monster (gives the emotion somewhere external to live)
→ Plan a restorative task for immediately after

Three steps. All of them doing work that the brain skips when it's already in avoidance mode.

What I got right: the recovery task is baked into the structure before you start.

Not as a reward. As a design requirement.

The task has a cost. The recovery is part of the plan.

If you skip the recovery, the next hard task is harder. That's not motivation. That's physics.

What I'm still figuring out:

How do you calibrate the monster to the task?

Not every hard task is the same level of emotional weight.

The Undead Ghoul felt right for the opossum.

But the system needs a way to scale.

That's the next design problem.

v1 is always like this. You solve one thing and three new questions appear.

label: The Quest Matrix mechanic was born from actually crawling under the house — showing how real-life friction becomes game design

posts.text:
Every hard task has a price tag.

Most people only plan for one line item.

Here are the five costs that actually matter:

1. Time

The only one anyone plans for.

Also the least important one on this list.

2. Emotional labor

Dread. Disgust. Anxiety. Overwhelm.

This is what keeps you from starting.

Not time. Not ability.

The emotional cost you didn't budget for.

3. Recovery time

After something hard, you need something restorative.

Not a reward. Maintenance.

Skip it and the next hard task costs more than it should.

4. Avoidance interest

Every day you don't do the thing, the dread compounds.

The task stays the same size.

The weight around it keeps growing.

5. Context debt

When you finally do the hard thing, you've often forgotten what came next.

Avoidance doesn't just cost dread.

It costs your thread of momentum.

The tasks that wreck us aren't the hard ones.

They're the ones we paid for twice:

Once in avoidance.
Once in the doing.

Plan for all five costs and the task is just a task.

Ignore them and every hard thing becomes a bigger deal than it needs to be.

label: Five things hard tasks actually cost that nobody puts in the plan
